data.title: Tilray outlines $850M-$900M revenue target for FY2025 amid strategic growth initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tilray Brands (TLRY) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Irwin Simon highlighted Tilray's transformation into a diversified consumer products company with a focus on cannabis, beverages, and wellness. He emphasized the potential of cannabis and beverage markets, noting that Tilray operates the largest cannabis business in Canada and leads in medical cannabis sales in Europe.
  • Management reported Q3 net revenue of $186 million, with a strategic focus on profitability and margin expansion rather than revenue growth at any cost. Adjusted net revenue, excluding strategic impacts, increased to $206 million.
  • The CEO noted margin improvements across segments, with a 5% increase in gross profit and a 200-basis-point increase in gross margin to 28% year-over-year.
  • CFO Carl Merton disclosed a $700 million non-cash impairment related to macroeconomic conditions and reduced regulatory expectations for cannabis legalization. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $9 million.
  • Tilray's beverage business generated $56 million in net revenue, with a gross margin increase to 36%. The Project 420 initiative is targeting $33 million in cost savings, with $20.6 million already achieved.

Outlook

  • Management revised fiscal 2025 revenue guidance to $850 million to $900 million, reflecting adjustments for strategic initiatives and SKU rationalization.
  • Plans for international market expansion include leveraging infrastructure to introduce brands in Europe and the UK, targeting higher-margin opportunities.
  • The company is focused on launching new cannabis products in Q4, including higher-THC medical cannabis flower in Germany, targeting increased profitability in international markets.

Financial Results

  • Q3 revenue was $185.8 million. Adjustments for strategic initiatives brought constant currency revenue to $193 million. Adjusted net revenue, factoring in strategic impacts, reached $206 million.
  • Cannabis net revenue was $54.3 million, with an 800-basis-point increase in gross margin to 41%, driven by international growth and strategic allocation of products to higher-margin markets.
  • Beverage net revenue stood at $56 million, supported by SKU rationalization and regional focus. Gross margin improved to 36%, up from 34% in the prior year.
  • Distribution and wellness segments contributed $61.5 million and $14.1 million in net revenue, respectively. The wellness segment saw an 8% growth in net revenue on a constant currency basis.

Q&A

  • Aaron Grey, Alliance Global Partners: Questioned the company's strategy for allocating cannabis products internationally and potential impacts on Canadian market share. CEO Simon emphasized profitability and focus on high-margin markets.
  • Victor Ma, TD Securities: Queried about cannabis gross margins and contributors to the 800-basis-point expansion. CFO Merton cited product mix and strategic focus on high-margin categories.
  • Frederico Gomes, ATB Capital Markets: Asked about pricing and competition in Germany. Chief Strategy Officer Denise Faltischek noted segmented pricing and stable insurance-based segments.
  • Pablo Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates: Inquired about extract market growth in Germany. Faltischek attributed growth to increased physician adoption and streamlined reimbursement processes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in Tilray's strategic decisions, particularly SKU rationalization and international expansion. Questions reflected cautious optimism about margin improvements and growth potential in key markets.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing profitability, strategic growth, and operational efficiencies. CEO Simon highlighted Tilray's resilience and long-term vision.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a stronger focus on profitability and strategic allocation of resources during the Q&A.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue guidance for FY2025 was revised downward from $950 million-$1 billion to $850 million-$900 million, reflecting strategic adjustments.
  • Cannabis gross margin improved significantly to 41% from 35% in Q2 2025, driven by international growth and higher-margin product focus.
  • Analysts' tone this quarter showed more interest in operational execution and cost-saving measures compared to last quarter's emphasis on revenue growth.
  • Management's tone remained consistent, with increased emphasis on profitability and addressing market challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted risks related to macroeconomic conditions and regulatory uncertainty, including delayed cannabis legalization in the U.S. and Europe.
  • Analysts raised concerns about competitive dynamics in international markets, particularly Germany, and the impact of SKU rationalization on near-term revenue.

Final Takeaway

Tilray Brands is focusing on strategic growth and profitability, with significant margin improvements and operational efficiencies. Despite revenue guidance adjustments, the company remains committed to expanding international markets, launching innovative products, and leveraging its diversified portfolio to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SA Asks: How will higher tariffs impact Japanese automakers?
data.description:
How will higher tariffs proposed by the Trump administration impact Japanese automakers?

Seeking Alpha analyst Caffital Research weighs in.

Caffital Research: Japanese automakers will likely see a significant negative impact from the 25% automotive tariff. Sales from the US market are highly important to the industry’s key manufacturers such as Toyota (NYSE:TM), Honda (NYSE:HMC) and Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY). The automotive tariff adds a significant cost item to the companies’ imported cars, essentially raising manufacturing costs and creating a structural disadvantage against domestic manufacturers in the US market. 

Although the major Japanese automakers produce cars in the US as well, they also depend on imported cars due to limited US manufacturing capacity. Additionally, even US manufacturing relies on auto parts that are partially sourced internationally, and such parts will be subject to tariffs.

In my opinion, there doesn't seem to be a clear comparative winner among the three major Japanese automakers. While Toyota (NYSE:TM), for example, produces the most cars in the US, all of the companies rely on imports in the market. The auto parts supply chains for US manufacturing are very complex, complicating pointing out a clear winner among the three.

Importantly, the tariff situation is still developing, and ongoing political negotiations may ultimately result in the tariffs being lifted or lowered at some point. Companies may also mitigate some of the impact by investing in increased US manufacturing capacity, though the uncertainty around tariffs currently makes such large long-term investments challenging to evaluate.

data.title: NewtekOne forecasts loans, deposits volumes at/above projections
data.description:
• Bank holding company NewtekOne (NASDAQ:NEWT) announced on Tuesday that its loan and deposit volumes are expected to be at or above projections.
  • The company originated 580 SBA 7((a)) loans totaling $213M during the first quarter, compared to the year-ago period's originations of 489 totaling $212M.
  • NewtekOne's non-bank subsidiary and Newtek Bank originated $17M of SBA 504 loans and executed $13M of SBA 504 loan sales.
  • The company originated $68.5M worth of loans under its Alternative Loan Program.
  • NEWT ended the quarter with ~$1.06B of deposits, up 2% sequentially and 88% year-over-year.
  • Business deposits rose 8.9%, core consumer deposit balances was up 2.5%, and wholesale deposits fell 32% from the prior quarter.
  • "As loan and deposit volumes are tracking at or above projections, we remain comfortable with 2025 EPS guidance of $2.10-$2.50 per share," said CEO Barry Sloane. The 2025 EPS consensus stands at $2.23.
  • NewtekOne is scheduled to announce its Q1 earnings on May 6.

data.title: SA Asks: Which of the Detroit Big 3 can best weather higher tariffs? (Update)
data.description:
Which of the Detroit Big Three automakers – Ford (NYSE:F), General Motors (NYSE:GM) and Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) – are best positioned to weather higher tariffs?

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Luca Socci and Sungarden Investment for their thoughts.

Luca Socci: When dealing with automakers, we have to keep in mind that it is one of the most globalized industries, with complex supply chains and continuous cross-border interactions. As a result, there really is no manufacturer that can claim to produce completely in one country. I think this partly explains why car components from Mexico and Canada are currently exempt from tariffs. 

However, if we consider the Detroit Big Three and what percentage of their vehicles sold in the U.S. comes from foreign countries, Ford (NYSE:F) seems to be below 20%, while General Motors (NYSE:GM) and Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) import around 40% of their sales. 

But when we consider momentum and pricing strategy, the picture changes. We just learned the preliminary Q1 2025 sales results, and GM (NYSE:GM) posted 17% growth. Ford's (NYSE:F) sales rose 5%, while Stellantis  (NYSE:STLA) reported a disappointing 12% drop. In the meantime, both Ford (F) and Stellantis (STLA) have announced aggressive pricing strategies, offering retail customers employee discounts. General Motors (GM), on the other hand, is confident of its pricing strategy and has not, so far, joined its main peers. 

What does this imply? It seems Ford (F) could be logistically better positioned, but from a profitability point of view, GM (GM) seems more in shape. Stellantis (STLA) is in the worst position right now, and until we know who the new CEO will be, I expect uncertainty and poor performance to continue. In short, I think GM (GM) could be the more balanced option.

I personally don't think that mid-tier automakers are the place to be invested right now. I have sold out of my car stocks and piled in Ferrari (NYSE:RACE). With tariffs, we have to hold companies that make unique products that can't go through the substitution effect.

Sungarden Investment: To put it bluntly, the best-positioned automaker for tariffs is Ford (F).

As we discussed in this article, in relative terms, Ford (F) does better than its main peers, General Motors (GM) and Stellantis (STLA), since it has a larger domestic footprint and 80% of the vehicles sold in the US are assembled in America. Meanwhile, General Motors (GM) and Stellantis (STLA) have plants in Mexico and Canada. So overall, Ford (F) should do better in the short term, and it will be easier to implement initiatives to mitigate these impacts, while the others will have to work harder to adapt to the scenario.

But the important word here is “relative”. Even Ford (F) will face considerable challenges, covering not only the direct impacts of tariffs such as increased costs, which can affect both revenue and margin, but also indirect impacts such as pressure on household incomes. As is well known, the automotive industry is very sensitive to these macroeconomic factors.

Another very important factor will be the ability to adapt. Ford (F) has already announced that it will make discounts for U.S. customers, and Stellantis (STLA) has mentioned that it will help suppliers with tariff payments. All of this will matter a lot in the medium term, as some things like the logistical adaptation to this new dynamic may take time to complete.

  • Top Automobile Manufacturers Stocks
  • Top Automotive Retail Stocks
  • Top Consumer Discretionary Stocks

data.title: Sell-off in Taiwan Semi likely 'overdone,' BofA says
data.description:
The sell-off in Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) shares is “likely overdone” at this point, Bank of America said on Tuesday, as the firm reiterated its Buy rating on the global foundry.

“With tariff concerns and AI supply chain hiccup baked in, TSMC’s valuation appears attractive at 14x/11.5x 2025/26E P/E, vs trough of 10x during the 2H22 semis downcycle amid heightened geopolitical tensions (e.g., Pelosi’s Taiwan visit),” analyst Brad Lin wrote in a note to clients. “We expect TSMC’s competitiveness and growth in 1H to remain on track, though tariff-related impact is inevitable.”

Lin reiterated his Buy rating on Taiwan Semiconductor but lowered his price target to $240 from $265.

Looking to the company's first-quarter results, Lin said he expects revenue to slip 4% sequentially and gross margin to come in at around 57.3%, at the low end of the company's guidance, due in part to earthquake disruptions.

There should be a rebound in the second-quarter, however, Lin added, as he expects revenue to grow 5.7% sequentially amid the continued ramp in artificial intelligence spending and increased shipments after the earthquake. The longer-term outlook is a little more muddy, due to worries about the supply chain and tariffs, Lin added.

Other potential issues that could be discussed include Taiwan Semiconductor's investment plans in the U.S., AI demand and the outlook for advanced packaging and any clarity on a potential joint venture with Intel (INTC).

data.title: LifeStance Health wins bullish view at KeyBanc on growth prospects
data.description:
• Shares of LifeStance Health (NASDAQ:LFST) gained on Tuesday after KeyBanc Capital Markets launched its coverage with an Overweight recommendation and a $9 price target, citing the behavioral healthcare firm’s growth prospects.
  • Analyst Steve Dechert projected Scottsdale, Arizona-based LifeStance (NASDAQ:LFST) to record mid-teens organic top-line growth over the next two years thanks mainly to higher visit volumes and better total revenue per visit driven by clinician additions and improved payor reimbursement rates, respectively.
  • The analyst also forecast the company to improve its margins due to factors such as optimized real estate footprint, higher clinician productivity, and ongoing cost efficiencies driven by technological advancements.
  • According to Dechert, LFST’s clear differentiator is its business strategy targeting in-person patient visits as well as virtual care, and the company’s acceptance of more than 150 different insurances could lead to higher patient retention, margins, and less revenue volatility.

data.title: Impact of U.S. import tariffs unclear on Woodside’s Louisiana LNG development, Goldman says
data.description:
The impact of U.S. import tariffs on capital spending for Woodside Energy's (NYSE:WDS) Louisiana LNG infrastructure development, in which Stonepeak has struck a deal for a 40% stake, is unclear, Goldman Sachs analysts Henry Meyer and Isaac Brooke said Tuesday.

There is a risk that Woodside (NYSE:WDS) may end up having to cover extra costs related to the new duties, the analysts said.

"We understand that Louisiana LNG is located within a Foreign Trade Zone that could be exempt from paying tariffs until goods enter the U.S. from the zone, Meyer and Brooke said, although "for Louisiana LNG, the tariffs may only be deferred until the project becomes operational."

While Woodside's (WDS) 40% sell-down is broadly neutral for its net present value, it offers timely de-gearing of the company's balance sheet against a weakening macroeconomic backdrop, Citi analyst Paul McTaggart wrote.

The deal "helps reduce funding pressure for other [Woodside] growth projects, though it also introduces longer-term exposure to potential cost overruns," according to McTaggart, who also thinks it may not be the only deal Woodside (WDS) signs for the megaproject, anticipating further announcements coming soon with the Bechtel "drop-dead date" looming in May.

data.title: Consumers to put more in their Easter basket this year
data.description:
Consumers are projected to spend 5% more on Easter this year compared to last, with total spending expected to reach $23.6 billion—just shy of the $24 billion record set in 2023, according to the National Retail Federation and Prosper Insights & Analytics.

This translates into $189.26 per person on average for those that celebrate Easter, $25.43 per person for those that do not.

Consumers will spend predominately on candy (92%), followed by food (89%), gifts (65%), decorations, including flowers (51%), and clothing (49%).

The most popular shopping destination remains discount stores (55%) while 44% will head to department stores. Thirty-six percent of those polled will do their shopping online, while 26% will go to small local businesses.

Related tickers: Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Target (TGT), TJX Companies (TJX), Macy’s (M), Ross Stores (ROST), Walmart (WMT), Nordstrom (JWN), Dillard’s (DDS), Dollar General (DG), Dollar Tree (DLTR), Hershey (HSY), Burlington Stores (BURL), Mondelez (MDLZ), Tootsie Roll (TR), Kohl’s (KSS).

data.title: Italy is said to plan expansion of defense budget amid U.S. pressure, NATO pledges
data.description:
Italy is preparing to unveil plans to boost its defense spending in order to meet NATO’s 2% gross domestic product target, with a formal announcement expected by the end of June, Bloomberg News reported Tuesday, citing sources familiar with the matter.

At present, Italy spends about 1.5% of its yearly economic output on defense, placing it below the alliance’s official threshold. However, officials are considering redefining what counts toward that target by including broader national security-related expenses, such as coast guard operations, to help close the gap.

The shift comes amid rising concerns over potential reductions in the U.S. military footprint in Italy, as Washington re-evaluates its global commitments. High-level discussions involving both military and political leaders are ongoing, sources said, and a final decision has not yet been made. A formal update is expected no later than the NATO summit scheduled for June 24-25.

The reassessment follows mounting pressure from President Donald Trump, now in his second term, who has long been critical of European countries failing to meet their defense spending obligations. Trump has called for NATO members to spend as much as 5% of GDP on defense, significantly above the current 2% goal.

Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, who may visit the White House as early as next week, has worked to maintain strong ties with the U.S. administration. Meloni has avoided publicly challenging Trump and has emphasized the importance of Italy’s strategic partnership with the United States, Bloomberg News reported.

Within Italy’s defense establishment, there is growing belief that NATO allies may eventually adopt a higher spending benchmark — possibly around 3.5% of GDP. That figure, insiders say, is what Italy would need to invest to compensate for a reduction in U.S. military support.

Nonetheless, questions remain about how such an increase would be financed, especially given Italy’s fiscally constrained budget. Raising defense spending by several billion euros annually would require major adjustments to public spending priorities.

The Italian government declined to comment to Bloomberg News on the ongoing discussions. However, with pressure from both NATO and Washington building, Rome appears ready to reframe its approach to defense spending — not just to meet alliance requirements, but also to safeguard its strategic standing in a rapidly evolving global security landscape.

data.title: RenaissanceRe upgraded, AIG downgraded at J.P. Morgan, among other moves
data.description:
J.P. Morgan upgraded RenaisssanceRe (NYSE:RNR) to Overweight on a less pessimistic view of reinsurance pricing, and downgraded American International Group (NYSE:AIG) to Neutral on the stock's outperformance and reductions in EPS estimates.

In small- to mid-cap names, the firm upgraded Palomar Holdings (NASDAQ:PLMR) to Overweight from Neutral and downgraded The Baldwin Insurance Group (NASDAQ:BWIN) to Neutral from Overweight.

For the P&C insurance sector overall, J.P. Morgan maintains a positive outlook as it expects pricing in most lines to remain firm and on the belief that re/insurers' balance sheets are healthy.

The bullish stance on RenaissanceRe (NYSE:RNR) is based on the expectation that reinsurance pricing will hold up better than feared in the near term, allowing RNR to generate robust ROE and strong book value growth, analyst Jimmy Bhullar wrote in a note to clients.

"Also, we believe that the company’s conservative approach with reserves makes it less susceptible to adverse reserve development, a key risk for major commercial lines re/insurers," he said.

The AIG downgrade stems from the stock's outperformance in the year-to-date as EPS estimates have declined. "Our fundamental outlook for AIG remains relatively upbeat given expected strong EPS growth, lower exposure to risks facing commercial lines insurers, and high capital flexibility. However, these factors seem better recognized in EPS forecasts, investor sentiment, and the stock’s valuation than at the time of our upgrade in October 2024," Bhullar said.

Regarding Palomar (NASDAQ:PLMR), the company is primed for strong growth, driven by strong gross premium growth and embedded growth in its reinsurance program.

In discussing the Baldwin (NASDAQ:BWIN) downgrade, J.P. Morgan believes there is more risk to the company's fundamental outlook than previously thought, due to the current macro backdrop. "New business growth in IAS/commercial P&C brokerage, which has been strong and has successfully offset the negative impacts of slower growth in pricing and exposure, could come under pressure in a weaker economic environment," the analyst aid.

The SA Quant system assigns a Hold rating to all four of the stocks. Meanwhile, the average Wall Street ratings for AIG (NYSE:AIG), Palomar (PLMR), and Baldwin (BWIN) are Buy, while the average Sell Side rating on RenaissanceRe (RNR) is Hold.
